questions.question:
For the year 2019, which of the previous year's calendar can be used?

A. 2011  B. 2012  C. 2013  D. 2014
questions.to_find: which previous year has the same calendar as 2019

understand.plain: Two years share the same calendar if they start on the same day of the week and both are either leap years or both are non-leap years. We need to find a year before 2019 whose January 1 falls on Tuesday (same as 2019) and which is also a non-leap year.

keywords.explain: Two years are calendar-equivalent when they begin on the same weekday and have the same total number of days (both 365 or both 366).
keywords.example: 2019 starts on Tuesday. We look for an earlier non-leap year also starting on Tuesday.

keywords.explain: Each ordinary year advances the weekday of Jan 1 by 1 day (365 = 52*7 + 1). Each leap year advances it by 2 days.
keywords.example: From 2013 to 2019: years 2013,2014,2015 give +1 each; 2016 (leap) gives +2; 2017,2018 give +1 each. Total = 3+2+1+1 = 7 days = 0 mod 7, so same start day.

keywords.explain: A year divisible by 4 is a leap year (366 days), unless it is a century year not divisible by 400.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; pure calendar arithmetic.

given.aim: Find a year prior to 2019 whose calendar (start weekday + leap/non-leap status) exactly matches 2019.

terms.meaning: 2019, a non-leap year that starts on Tuesday (January 1, 2019 was a Tuesday).
terms.example: Every date in 2019 falls on the same weekday as the corresponding date in any equivalent year.
terms.connects: We need another non-leap year starting on Tuesday.

terms.meaning: The number of days beyond complete weeks in a year; 1 for ordinary years, 2 for leap years.
terms.connects: Summing odd days from 2013 to 2018 gives 7, which is 0 mod 7.

terms.meaning: To find when a calendar repeats, accumulate odd days until the total is a multiple of 7.
terms.example: Starting from 2013, after adding odd days for 2013 through 2018 we get 1+1+1+2+1+1=7, so 2019 repeats 2013.
terms.connects: 2013 is a non-leap year starting on Tuesday, same as 2019.

given.plan: Count the total odd days from the candidate year up to (but not including) 2019. If the total is divisible by 7 and both years have the same type (leap/non-leap), the calendars match.

steps.title: Identify 2019 properties
steps.formula_raw: 2019 mod 4 != 0 => non-leap year, starts on Tuesday
steps.apply: 2019 is a non-leap year (365 days). January 1, 2019 was a Tuesday. We need another non-leap year also starting on a Tuesday.

steps.title: Count odd days from 2013 to 2018 (6 years before 2019)
steps.formula_raw: odd days = 1 per ordinary year, 2 per leap year
steps.note: A total of 0 mod 7 means 2019 starts on the same weekday as 2013.

steps.title: Verify 2013 is also a non-leap year
steps.apply: 2013 is a non-leap year (365 days), same as 2019. Both start on Tuesday. Therefore the calendars are identical.
steps.note: Check other options: 2011 starts on Saturday, 2012 is a leap year (different), 2014 starts on Wednesday. Only 2013 matches.

solution.result: The year 2013 has the same calendar as 2019. Answer: C. 2013.

questions.formula_note: Calendar equivalence uses modular arithmetic on odd-day accumulation; no formula from the registry directly covers this topic.

questions.question:
A person was born on the fifth Monday of February in a particular year. Which one of the following statements is correct based on the above information?

A. The 2nd February of that year is a Tuesday
B. There will be five Sundays in the month of February in that year
C. The 1st February of that year is a Sunday
D. All Mondays of February in that year have even dates
questions.answer: A. The 2nd February of that year is a Tuesday
questions.to_find: correct statement implied by February having a 5th Monday

understand.plain: February has 5 Mondays only in a leap year (29 days) when it starts on Monday, or in a non-leap year (28 days) — but 28 = 4*7, so a non-leap February has exactly 4 of each weekday. So February must have 29 days and start on Monday. This means February 1 is a Monday, and we can work out every date's weekday.

keywords.explain: For February to have a 5th Monday, it must have at least 29 days (leap year). February 1 must be a Monday so that Mondays fall on 1, 8, 15, 22, 29.
keywords.example: If Feb 1 is Monday, the Mondays are 1, 8, 15, 22, 29 — giving exactly 5 Mondays in a 29-day February.

keywords.explain: February has 29 days in a leap year. For 5 occurrences of any weekday, the month must start on that weekday.
keywords.example: If Feb 1 is Monday in a leap year: 5 Mondays (1,8,15,22,29), 4 each of all other days.

keywords.explain: From a known weekday on date d, we find the weekday of date d+k by shifting k days forward.
keywords.example: Feb 1 = Monday => Feb 2 = Tuesday => Feb 8 = Monday => Feb 29 = 5th Monday.

height='28' class='hi'/><text x='38' y='123' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>8</text><rect x='70' y='104' width='56' height='28' class='bg'/><text x='98' y='123' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>9</text><rect x='10' y='136' width='56' height='28' class='hi'/><text x='38' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>15</text><rect x='10' y='168' width='56' height='28' class='hi'/><text x='38' y='187' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>22</text><rect x='10' y='200' width='56' height='28' class='hi'/><text x='38' y='219' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>29</text><text x='250' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>5 Mondays: 1,8,15,22,29</text><text x='250' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Feb 2 = Tuesday</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Calendar grid for a leap-year February starting on Monday, with Mondays highlighted showing five Mondays on dates 1, 8, 15, 22, 29.

given.aim: Determine which statement about the month of February is necessarily true given that the 5th Monday exists.

terms.meaning: For 5 Mondays to exist in February, the month must have 29 days (leap year) and start on Monday.
terms.example: If Feb had 28 days, it contains exactly 4 of every weekday regardless of the starting day.
terms.connects: Forces Feb 1 = Monday and the year to be a leap year.

terms.meaning: The only way to pack 5 Mondays into 29 days is to have Mondays on 1, 8, 15, 22, 29.
terms.example: Any other starting weekday gives at most 4 Mondays in a 29-day February.
terms.connects: Determines the weekday of every other date in that February.

terms.meaning: If Feb 1 is Monday, then Feb 2 is the very next day, which is Tuesday.
terms.example: Monday + 1 day = Tuesday. This is statement A.
terms.connects: Directly verifies option A as the correct answer.

given.plan: Establish that Feb 1 must be Monday (the only configuration giving 5 Mondays in a 29-day February), then check each statement.

steps.title: Determine the minimum length of February
steps.apply: A 28-day February has exactly 4 occurrences of each weekday. For 5 Mondays, February must have 29 days (leap year). With 29 days: the three extra days (beyond 28 = 4 weeks) must include Monday. So Feb 1 must be Monday.
steps.note: If Feb 1 were any other day, Mondays would fall on 2,9,16,23 or later, giving only 4 Mondays.

steps.title: Identify Feb 2's weekday
steps.apply: Feb 1 = Monday => Feb 2 = Tuesday. Statement A says '2nd February is a Tuesday' — this is true.

steps.title: Check other options
steps.apply: B: Sundays fall on 7,14,21,28 — only 4 Sundays. False. C: Feb 1 is Monday, not Sunday. False. D: Monday dates are 1,8,15,22,29 — includes 1 and 15, which are odd. False.

solution.result: Feb 1 is Monday in a leap year, so Feb 2 is Tuesday. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: Calendar weekday arithmetic uses basic modular arithmetic; no specific registry formula applies here.

questions.question:
Two straight lines are drawn perpendicular to each other in the X-Y plane. If alpha and beta are the acute angles the straight lines make with the X-axis, then alpha + beta = ?

A. 60 degrees  B. 90 degrees  C. 120 degrees  D. 180 degrees
questions.to_find: the value of alpha + beta when two perpendicular lines make acute angles alpha and beta with the X-axis

understand.plain: Two lines that are perpendicular to each other differ in slope such that the product of their slopes is -1. If one line makes an acute angle alpha with the X-axis, the perpendicular line makes an angle of (90 + alpha) or equivalently its acute angle with the X-axis is (90 - alpha). So beta = 90 - alpha, giving alpha + beta = 90.

keywords.explain: Two lines are perpendicular if and only if their slopes m1 and m2 satisfy m1 * m2 = -1 (or one is vertical).
keywords.example: A line with slope 1 (angle 45 degrees) is perpendicular to a line with slope -1 (angle 135 degrees, acute angle 45 degrees with negative X direction, or equivalently 90-45=45 as the acute angle measured from X-axis).

keywords.explain: The acute angle a line makes with the positive X-axis is the smaller of the two supplementary angles; it is always between 0 and 90 degrees.
keywords.example: A line with slope -2 makes an obtuse angle with positive X-axis but an acute angle of arctan(2) ~ 63.4 degrees with the negative X-axis direction.

keywords.explain: If a line makes angle theta with the positive X-axis, its slope m = tan(theta). For a perpendicular line, the angle shifts by 90 degrees.
keywords.example: Line 1: angle alpha, slope = tan(alpha). Perpendicular line: angle = 90 + alpha (obtuse with positive X), acute angle with X-axis = 180 - (90+alpha) = 90 - alpha = beta.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 340 260' width='340' height='260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='340' height='260' rx='10' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><line x1='20' y1='130' x2='320' y2='130' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='170' y1='20' x2='170' y2='240' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='315' y='126' class='sub ink'>X</text><text x='174' y='22' class='sub ink'>Y</text><line x1='60' y1='210' x2='280' y2='70' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='60' y1='70' x2='280' y2='210' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='285' y='68' class='sub mut'>L1</text><text x='285' y='215' class='sub mut'>L2</text><path d='M 215 130 A 45 45 0 0 0 190 108' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='218' y='122' class='sub mut' style='fill:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)'>alpha</text><path d='M 125 130 A 45 45 0 0 1 150 108' fill='none' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='92' y='122' class='sub mut' style='fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75)'>beta</text><text x='170' y='255' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>alpha + beta = 90 degrees</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two perpendicular lines crossing the X-axis, each making an acute angle with the positive X-axis; together alpha and beta sum to 90 degrees.

given.aim: Find alpha + beta, where alpha and beta are the acute angles that two mutually perpendicular lines make with the X-axis.

terms.meaning: L1 makes an acute angle alpha with the positive X-axis, so its slope m1 = tan(alpha) where 0 < alpha < 90.
terms.example: If alpha = 60 degrees, m1 = tan(60) = sqrt(3).
terms.connects: Used to derive the slope of the perpendicular line.

terms.meaning: For two lines with slopes m1 and m2 to be perpendicular: m1 * m2 = -1.
terms.example: If m1 = sqrt(3), then m2 = -1/sqrt(3), meaning L2 has negative slope.
terms.connects: The negative slope means L2 makes an obtuse angle with the positive X-axis.

terms.meaning: If L2 has negative slope, its angle with positive X-axis is obtuse (180 - beta). The acute angle is the supplement, beta.
terms.example: m2 = -1/sqrt(3) => angle = 150 degrees => acute angle beta = 180 - 150 = 30 degrees.

given.plan: Use the perpendicularity slope condition m1*m2 = -1, relate slopes to angles via tan, and find alpha + beta.

steps.title: Set up slopes in terms of angles
steps.formula_raw: m1 = tan(alpha), m2 = tan(theta2) where theta2 is the angle L2 makes with positive X-axis
steps.apply: L1 makes acute angle alpha, so m1 = tan(alpha). Since L2 is perpendicular to L1, m1 * m2 = -1, so m2 = -1/tan(alpha) = -cot(alpha) = -tan(90 - alpha).
steps.note: The negative sign means L2 makes an obtuse angle with the positive X-axis.

steps.title: Find the acute angle beta of L2
steps.apply: Comparing: -tan(beta) = -tan(90 - alpha) => tan(beta) = tan(90 - alpha) => beta = 90 - alpha.
steps.note: Since alpha is acute (0 < alpha < 90), beta = 90 - alpha is also in (0, 90), confirming it is an acute angle.

steps.title: Compute the sum
steps.apply: alpha + beta = 90 degrees, regardless of the specific value of alpha.
steps.note: This holds for any pair of perpendicular lines that are not horizontal or vertical.

solution.result: alpha + beta = 90 degrees. Answer: B. 90 degrees.

questions.formula_note: The perpendicular slope condition m1*m2 = -1 and the tangent angle relationship are standard coordinate geometry results not separately listed in the registry.

questions.question:
A function g(z) is defined in the interval [0, 1] on the z-axis as:

  g(z) = { 2   if  0 <= z < 1/2
           { 3   if  1/2 <= z < 1
           { 2   if  z = 1

Which one of the following is the area under the curve for the interval [0, 1] on the z-axis?

A. 1/2  B. 3/4  C. 5/2  D. 3/2
questions.to_find: area under the piecewise constant curve g(z) over the interval [0, 1]

understand.plain: The function g(z) is piecewise constant: it equals 2 on [0, 1/2), equals 3 on [1/2, 1), and equals 2 at the single point z=1. The area under a piecewise constant function is simply the sum of (value * width) for each sub-interval. Since single points have zero width, the point z=1 contributes nothing.

keywords.explain: A function that takes a fixed value on each sub-interval of its domain.
keywords.example: g(z) = 2 on [0, 0.5) and g(z) = 3 on [0.5, 1) creates a step function with one jump.

keywords.explain: For a piecewise constant function, the area equals the sum of (function value) * (sub-interval length) for each piece.
A single point has zero length (measure zero), so it contributes zero to an integral.
keywords.example: The value g(1) = 2 at a single point does not affect the integral over [0, 1].

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 300 200' width='300' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe y='164' text-anchor='end' class='sub mut'>0</text><text x='155' y='174' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>1/2</text><text x='270' y='174' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>1</text><text x='30' opacity='0.6'/><rect x='155' y='80' width='115' height='40' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke='none' opacity='0.4'/><line x1='40' y1='120' x2='155' y2='120' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' fill='none' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='155' cy='120' r='4' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='97' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>2 x 1/2 = 1</text><text x='212' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>3 x 1/2 = 3/2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Step function graph: g=2 on [0, 1/2) shown as a purple bar, g=3 on [1/2, 1) shown as a green bar; total area = 1 + 3/2 = 5/2.

given.aim: Compute the definite integral (area) of the piecewise function g(z) over [0, 1].

terms.meaning: g(z) = 2 for all z in [0, 0.5). This is a constant function at height 2 over a half-unit interval.
g(z) = 3 for all z in [0.5, 1). Constant function at height 3 over a half-unit interval.
g(1) = 2, but a single point has zero measure, so it adds nothing to the integral.
terms.example: A point has no width, so height * 0 = 0.
terms.connects: Does not change the total area.

given.plan: Split the integral at z = 1/2 and compute each piece as a rectangle, then add them.

steps.title: Integrate over [0, 1/2)
steps.note: The single point at z = 0 is included; it does not affect the integral value.

steps.title: Integrate over [1/2, 1)
steps.note: The endpoint z=1 is excluded from this piece but covered separately.

steps.title: Add contributions
steps.note: The single point g(1) = 2 contributes zero measure, so it does not affect the result.

solution.result: Area under g(z) on [0, 1] = 5/2. Answer: C. 5/2.

questions.formula_note: The area computation uses basic integration of a step function as a sum of rectangles; no specific registry formula is needed.

questions.question:
Two straight lines pass through the origin (x_0, y_0) = (0, 0). One of them passes through the point (x_1, y_1) = (1, 3) and the other passes through the point (x_2, y_2) = (1, 2). What is the area enclosed between the straight lines in the interval [0, 1] on the x-axis?

A. 0.5  B. 1.0  C. 1.5  D. 2.0
questions.to_find: area enclosed between two lines through the origin over the interval [0, 1] on the x-axis

understand.plain: Both lines pass through the origin, so their equations are y = m*x. One passes through (1, 3) so its slope is 3; the other passes through (1, 2) so its slope is 2. The area between them from x=0 to x=1 is the integral of the difference of the two functions: integral from 0 to 1 of (3x - 2x) dx = integral of x dx = 1/2.

keywords.explain: A line through (0,0) and (a, b) has equation y = (b/a)*x, with slope m = b/a.
The area between f(x) and g(x) on [a,b] (where f >= g) is the integral from a to b of (f(x) - g(x)) dx.
keywords.example: Between y=3x and y=2x on [0,1]: integral of (3x-2x)dx = integral of x dx.

keywords.explain: The integral from 0 to 1 of x dx = [x^2/2] from 0 to 1 = 1/2.
keywords.example: This is the area of the triangle with vertices (0,0), (1,0), and (1,1).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 280 220' width='280' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='280' height='220' rx='10' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><line x1='30' y1='180' x2='250' y2='180' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='30' y1='180' x2='30' y2='20' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='255' y='184' class='sub ink'>x</text><text x='22' y='22' class='sub ink'>y</text><text x='25' y='184' text-anchor='end' class='sub mut'>0</text><text x='230' y='194' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>1</text><polygon points='30,180 230,180 230,30' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' opacity='0.5'/><polygon points='30,180 230,180 230,80' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' opacity='0.8'/><line x1='30' y1='180' x2='230' y2='30' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='30' y1='180' x2='230' y2='80' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='235' y='32' class='sub mut' style='fill:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)'>y=3x</text><text x='235' y='82' class='sub mut' style='fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75)'>y=2x</text><text x='185' y='100' class='sub mut'>Area</text><text x='185' y='116' class='sub mut'>= 0.5</text><circle cx='230' cy='30' r='4' fill='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)'/><circle cx='230' cy='80' r='4' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)'/><text x='235' y='178' class='sub mut'>(1,0)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two lines from the origin: y=3x (steeper) and y=2x (less steep), with the triangular area between them over [0,1] shaded in purple, equal to 0.5.

given.aim: Find the area of the region between lines y=3x and y=2x for x in [0,1].

terms.connects: Upper boundary of the enclosed region.
Lower boundary of the enclosed region.

terms.meaning: The vertical gap between the two lines is 3x - 2x = x for all x in [0,1].
terms.connects: Integrating this gap over [0,1] gives the enclosed area.

given.plan: Write equations of both lines, subtract to find the gap, then integrate the gap from 0 to 1.

steps.title: Find equations of both lines
steps.formula_raw: y = (y1/x1)*x for a line through origin and (x1,y1)
steps.apply: Line through (0,0) and (1,3): slope = 3/1 = 3, so y = 3x. Line through (0,0) and (1,2): slope = 2/1 = 2, so y = 2x.
steps.note: Since both lines start at the origin, they only intersect at x=0 and diverge for x>0.

steps.title: Set up the area integral
steps.formula_raw: Area = integral_{0}^{1} (f(x) - g(x)) dx = integral_{0}^{1} (3x - 2x) dx
steps.apply: For x in [0,1]: 3x >= 2x, so the upper curve is y=3x and lower is y=2x. Area = integral from 0 to 1 of x dx.
steps.note: The integrand simplifies beautifully to just x.

steps.title: Evaluate the integral
steps.note: This corresponds to the area of the triangle with base 1 and height 1 (the gap at x=1 is 3-2=1).

solution.result: Area enclosed between y=3x and y=2x over [0,1] = 0.5. Answer: A. 0.5.

questions.formula_note: Area between two curves uses definite integration of the difference; this is a standard calculus result not separately listed in the registry.
